title: Do Music Lessons Really Make Kids Smarter?
subtitle: 音乐课真能提高智商吗

paragraphs.en: In 2004, a paper appeared in the journal Psychological Science, titled 'Music Lessons Enhance IQ.' The author, composer and psychologist Glenn Schellenberg had conducted an experiment with 144 children randomly assigned to four groups. One learned the keyboard for a year, one took singing lessons, one joined an acting class, and a control group had no extracurricular training.
paragraphs.zh: 2004年，《心理科学》期刊发表题为《音乐课提升智商》的论文。作者、作曲家兼心理学家格伦·谢伦伯格对随机分为四组的144名儿童进行实验：一组学键盘一年，一组上声乐课，一组参加表演班，对照组无课外培训。

paragraphs.en: The IQ of the children in the two musical groups rose by an average of seven points in the course of a year; those in the other two groups gained an average of 4.3 points. Schellenberg had long been skeptical of the science supporting claims that music education enhances children's abstract reasoning, math, or language skills. If children who play the piano are smarter, he says, it doesn't necessarily mean they are smarter because they play the piano.
paragraphs.zh: 两个音乐组儿童的智商一年内平均提高7分，另两组平均提高4.3分。谢伦伯格长期质疑音乐教育能提升儿童抽象推理、数学或语言技能的科学论断。他说，弹钢琴的孩子更聪明，未必是因为弹钢琴而变聪明。

paragraphs.en: It could be that the youngsters who play the piano also happen to be more ambitious or better at focusing on a task. Correlation, after all, does not prove causation. The 2004 paper was specifically designed to address those concerns. And as a passionate musician, Schellenberg was delighted when he turned up credible evidence that music has transfer effects on general intelligence.
paragraphs.zh: 可能是学钢琴的孩子本就更有抱负或更专注。相关性毕竟不等于因果。2004年论文正是为回应这些疑虑而设计。作为热爱音乐之人，谢伦伯格很高兴自己发现了音乐对一般智力有迁移效应的可信证据。

paragraphs.en: But nearly a decade later, in 2013, the Education Endowment Foundation funded a bigger study with more than 900 students. That study failed to confirm Schellenberg's findings, producing no evidence that music lessons improved math and literacy skills. Schellenberg took that news in stride while continuing to cast a skeptical eye on the research in his field.
paragraphs.zh: 但近十年后的2013年，教育捐赠基金会资助了一项涉及900多名学生的更大研究，却未能证实谢伦伯格的发现，没有证据表明音乐课能提升数学和读写能力。谢伦伯格泰然处之，并继续以怀疑眼光审视本领域研究。

paragraphs.en: Recently, he decided to formally investigate just how often his fellow researchers in psychology and neuroscience make what he believes are erroneous—or at least premature—causal connections between music and intelligence. His results, published in May, suggest that many of his peers do just that. For his recent study, Schellenberg asked two research assistants to look for correlational studies on the effects of music education.
paragraphs.zh: 最近他决定正式调查心理学和神经科学领域的同行们有多频繁地在音乐与智力之间建立他认为是错误或至少草率的因果关联。五月发表的结果显示，许多同行确实如此。在此次研究中，他让两名助理搜集关于音乐教育效果的相关性研究。

paragraphs.en: They found a total of 114 papers published since 2000. To assess whether the authors claimed any causation, researchers then looked for telltale verbs in each paper's title and abstract, verbs like 'enhance,' 'promote,' 'facilitate,' and 'strengthen.' The papers were categorized as neuroscience if the study employed a brain imaging method like magnetic resonance, or if the study appeared in a journal that had 'brain,' 'neuroscience,' or a related term in its title. Otherwise the papers were categorized as psychology.
paragraphs.zh: 他们共找到2000年以来发表的114篇论文。为评估作者是否宣称因果，研究人员查找每篇论文标题和摘要中的标志性动词，如“增强”、“促进”、“有助于”、“强化”。若研究采用核磁共振等脑成像方法，或发表于标题含“脑”、“神经科学”等词的期刊，则归类为神经科学，其余归为心理学。

words.meaning: adj. 暴露实情的，说明问题的

paragraphs.en: Schellenberg didn't tell his assistants what exactly he was trying to prove. After computing their assessments, Schellenberg concluded that the majority of the articles erroneously claimed that music training had a causal effect. The overselling, he also found, was more prevalent among neuroscience studies, three quarters of which mischaracterized a mere association between music training and skills enhancement as a cause-and-effect relationship.
paragraphs.zh: 谢伦伯格未告知助手具体要证明什么。综合评估后他得出结论：大多数文章错误宣称音乐训练具有因果效应。他还发现过度宣传在神经科学研究中更普遍，四分之三将音乐训练与技能提升的单纯关联曲解为因果关系。

paragraphs.en: This may come as a surprise to some. Psychologists have been battling charges that they don't do 'real' science for some time—in large part because many findings from classic experiments have proved unreproducible. Neuroscientists, on the other hand, armed with brain scans and EEGs, have not been subject to the same degree of critique.
paragraphs.zh: 这或许令一些人惊讶。心理学家长期与“不做真科学”的指责抗争，很大程度上因许多经典实验发现无法重现。而神经科学家凭借脑扫描和脑电图，尚未遭受同等批评。

paragraphs.en: To argue for a cause-and-effect relationship, scientists must attempt to explain why and how a connection could occur. When it comes to transfer effects of music, scientists frequently point to brain plasticity—the fact that the brain changes according to how we use it. When a child learns to play the violin, for example, several studies have shown that the brain region responsible for the fine motor skills of the left hand's fingers is likely to grow.
paragraphs.zh: 要论证因果关系，科学家必须解释联系为何及如何发生。谈及音乐迁移效应时，科学家常指向大脑可塑性，即大脑随使用方式而改变。例如孩子学小提琴时，多项研究表明负责左手手指精细运动技能的脑区会增长。

paragraphs.en: And many experiments have shown that musical training improves certain hearing capabilities, like filtering voices from background noise or distinguishing the difference between the consonants 'b' and 'g'. But Schellenberg remains highly critical of how the concept of plasticity has been applied in his field. 'Plasticity has become an industry of its own,' he wrote in his May paper.
paragraphs.zh: 许多实验也表明音乐训练能改善特定听觉能力，如从背景噪音中筛选人声或区分辅音“b”和“g”。但谢伦伯格对可塑性概念在本领域的应用方式持严厉批评。他在五月论文中写道：“可塑性已自成一派产业。”

paragraphs.en: Practice does change the brain, he allows, but what is questionable is the assertion that these changes affect other brain regions, such as those responsible for spatial reasoning or math problems. Neuropsychologist Lutz Jancke agrees. 'Most of these studies don't allow for causal inferences,' he said.
paragraphs.zh: 他承认练习确实改变大脑，但可疑的是这些变化会影响其他脑区（如负责空间推理或数学解题的区域）。神经心理学家卢茨·扬克表示赞同，称“这些研究大多无法得出因果推论”。

paragraphs.en: For over two decades, Jancke has researched the effects of music lessons, and like Schellenberg, he believes that the only way to truly understand their effects is to run longitudinal studies. In such studies, researchers would need to follow groups of children with and without music lessons over a long period of time—even if the assignments are not completely random. Then they could compare outcomes for each group.
paragraphs.zh: 二十多年来，扬克一直研究音乐课效果。和谢伦伯格一样，他认为真正了解其效果的唯一途径是开展纵向研究。在这类研究中，研究人员需长期追踪有音乐课和无音乐课的儿童群体，即便分组并非完全随机，然后比较各组结果。

paragraphs.en: Some researchers are starting to do just that. The neuroscientist Peter Schneider from Heidelberg University in Germany, for example, has been following a group of children for ten years now. Some of them were handed musical instruments and given lessons through a school-based program in the Ruhr region of Germany called Jedem Kind ein Instrument, or 'an instrument for every child,' which was carried out with government funding.
paragraphs.zh: 一些研究者已开始这样做。例如德国海德堡大学的神经科学家彼得·施奈德已追踪一组儿童长达十年。其中部分孩子通过德国鲁尔区一项政府资助的校内项目“每个孩子一件乐器”获得了乐器和课程。

paragraphs.en: Among these children, Schneider has found that those who were enthusiastic about music and who practiced voluntarily showed improvements in hearing ability, as well as in more general competencies, such as the ability to concentrate. To establish whether effects such as improved concentration are caused by music participation itself, and not by investing time in an extracurricular activity of any kind, Assal Habibi, a psychology professor at the University of Southern California, is conducting a five-year longitudinal study with children from low-income communities in Los Angeles.
paragraphs.zh: 施奈德发现，这些孩子中对音乐有热情且主动练习者，其听力及注意力等一般能力均有提升。为确定注意力提升等效果是源于音乐参与本身而非投入任何课外活动的时间，南加州大学心理学教授阿萨尔·哈比比正对洛杉矶低收入社区儿童进行为期五年的纵向研究。

paragraphs.en: The youngsters fall into three groups: those who take after-school music, those who do after-school sports, and those with no structured after-school program at all. After two years, Habibi and her colleagues reported seeing structural changes in the brains of the musically trained children, both locally and in the pathways connecting different parts of the brain. That may seem compelling, but Habibi's children were not selected randomly.
paragraphs.zh: 这些儿童分为三组：课后学音乐者、课后做运动者及无任何结构课后项目者。两年后，哈比比及同事报告称，受过音乐训练的儿童大脑在局部区域和连接不同脑区的通路上均出现结构性变化。这看似有力，但哈比比的儿童并非随机选取。

words.meaning: adj. 令人信服的，有说服力的

paragraphs.en: Did the children who were drawn to music perhaps have something in them from the start that made them different but eluded the brain scanners? 'As somebody who started taking piano lessons at the age of five and got up every morning at seven to practice, that experience changed me and made me part of who I am today,' Schellenberg said. 'The question is whether those kinds of experiences do so systematically across individuals and create exactly the same changes.'
paragraphs.zh: 被音乐吸引的孩子是否从一开始就有某种与众不同却未被脑扫描仪捕捉的特质？谢伦伯格说：“作为五岁开始学钢琴、每天七点起床练琴的人，那段经历改变了我，塑造了今天的我。问题是这类经历能否在个体间系统地产生完全相同的变化。”

paragraphs.en: 'And I think that is that huge leap of faith.' Did he have a hidden talent that others didn't have? Or more endurance than his peers? Music researchers tend, like Schellenberg, to be musicians themselves, and as he noted in his recent paper, 'the idea of positive cognitive and neural side effects from music training (and other pleasurable activities) is inherently appealing.'
paragraphs.zh: “我认为那是信仰的巨大飞跃。”他是否有他人不具备的隐藏天赋？还是比同龄人更有耐力？音乐研究者常如谢伦伯格般本身也是音乐人。正如他在近期论文中所说，“音乐训练及其他愉悦活动能带来积极的认知和神经副作用这一想法本身具有内在吸引力。”

paragraphs.en: He also admits that if he had children of his own, he would encourage them to take music lessons and go to university. 'I would think that it makes them better people, more critical, just wiser in general,' he said. But those convictions should be checked at the entrance to the lab, he added. Otherwise, the work becomes religion or faith. 'You have to let go of your faith if you want to be a scientist.'
paragraphs.zh: 他也承认若自己有孩子，会鼓励他们学音乐和上大学。“我认为这能让他们成为更好的人，更具批判性，整体更有智慧。”但他补充，这些信念应在进入实验室时被检视，否则工作就变成了宗教或信仰。“想成为科学家，就必须放下信仰。”
